content.problemStatement:
Sonu and Titu are playing a tennis match. They are playing a "best of NNN sets" match (NNN is always odd). That means that they will play at most NNN sets, and the person who has won most sets wins the match. But they are smart, and if they notice at any point that one of them has no chance of winning the match, they will stop the match right then, without playing out all the NNN sets needlessly.

For example, suppose N=11N = 11N=11. And at some point, Sonu has won 5 sets, and Titu has won 4 sets. Sonu is leading now, but it is possible that Titu wins the two remaining sets and wins the whole match. So they will not stop right now.

But suppose Sonu wins the next set as well, and so now the score is (6,4)(6, 4)(6,4). Now, even though there is one set remaining, there is no chance of Titu winning the whole match, since at best, even if he wins the next set, the score will become (6,5)(6, 5)(6,5), and Sonu still wins. So, they will stop at a score of (6,4)(6, 4)(6,4) and declare Sonu as the winner.

As another example, if N=15N=15N=15, and the score is currently (8,2)(8, 2)(8,2), then they'll stop right now, since there is no way for Titu to win the match. Even if Titu wins all the 555 remaining sets, Titu cannot win the match.

You are watching the match, and you see that the match has ended with a score of (X,Y)(X, Y)(X,Y). That is, Sonu has won XXX sets, and Titu has won YYY sets. But you don't know what NNN is. Can you find out NNN from just knowing the final score?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input containing two space-separated integers XXX and YYY where XXX is the number of sets won by Sonu and YYY is the number of sets won by Titu.


Output Format

For each test case, output on a new line the value of NNN.
Note that you should only output an odd integer.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers X and Y — the number of sets won by Sonu and Titu respectively (X ≠ Y).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single odd integer N on its own line — the total number of sets that were scheduled for the match.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each pair (X,Y) we compute W = max(X,Y) and S = X+Y. The answer is max( smallest odd >= S , 2*W-1 ). This yields 9, 19, 197 and 15 respectively.

solution.approach:
Let W = max(X, Y) be the winner's sets and L = min(X, Y) the loser’s sets. The match stops exactly when the loser cannot possibly overtake the winner even if he wins all remaining sets. If the total scheduled sets is N, the remaining sets after the final score are N‑(X+Y). The stopping condition is L + (N‑(X+Y)) < W, i.e. N < 2·W. The match must not have stopped earlier, which forces N to be at least 2·W‑1 (the smallest odd number that makes the inequality true for the first time). Therefore the answer is the smallest odd N such that N ≥ X+Y and N ≥ 2·W‑1. Since 2·W‑1 is always odd, the answer can be computed as:
    oddS = (X+Y) if (X+Y) is odd else (X+Y)+1
    N = max(oddS, 2*W-1)
The algorithm runs in O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        X = int(next(it)); Y = int(next(it))
        W = max(X, Y)
        S = X + Y
        oddS = S if S % 2 == 1 else S + 1
        N = max(oddS, 2 * W - 1)
        out.append(str(N))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
